16: pasa graphe theopneustos kai ophelimos (pāsa graphē theopneustos kai ōphelimos): 'All Scripture is God-breathed (theopneustos) and profitable.' Theopneustos is a Pauline coinage (hapax legomenon) combining theos (God) and pneō (breathe) — 'God-breathed' or 'breathed out by God.' The breath-metaphor echoes Genesis 2:7 (YHWH breathed into Adam's nostrils) and the Spirit-wind of Ezekiel's valley of bones: the Scriptures are alive with divine breath, not merely human compositions. The primary referent is the OT (which Timothy was taught from childhood, 2 Timothy 3:15); the claim's extension to apostolic writings is implicit in 2 Peter 3:16's treatment of Paul's letters as graphe.
1: En eschatais hēmerais (in the last days): the eschatological temporal marker that frames the entire vice list of 2 Timothy 3:1-9. The "last days" in Jewish and early Christian thought referred to the age inaugurated by the Messiah's coming — Paul consistently treats the present age as the "last days" (1 Corinthians 10:11; Hebrews 1:2). Enestēsontai kairoi chalepoi (difficult/dangerous times will come) — chalepos = difficult, dangerous, fierce; the same adjective used in Matthew 8:28 for the Gadarene demoniacs who were too fierce to pass. The "last days" bring not only eschatological glory but eschatological conflict.
2: The vice list of 2 Timothy 3:2-4 is one of the densest in the NT. Philautoi (self-lovers) — the compound philos + autos; the Stoic tradition condemned philautia as the root of moral failure. Philargyroi (money-lovers) — the same compound structure as philautia; love of self and love of money are the twin root vices. Alazones (boastful/swaggering) — those who claim more than they possess; hyperēphanoi (arrogant/overweening) — those who look down on others. Blasphēmoi (slanderous, abusive) — applied here to speech that injures human honor rather than divine honor. Goneusin apeitheis (disobedient to parents) — the violation of the fifth commandment, which the Stoics also placed high among social obligations.
3: Astorgoi (without natural affection/family love): storgē is the specific term for the natural love of family members (especially parents and children); its absence marks the breakdown of the most basic social bond. Aspondoi (irreconcilable, without truce) — those who will not negotiate even a temporary truce; the term comes from the practice of spondai (libations poured at treaty-making). Diaboloi (slanderous, devil-like) — the same word as "devil" (diabolos = the slanderer); slanderers act in the devil's character. Akrateis (without self-control) — the opposite of enkrateia (self-control), a key Stoic and Pastoral Epistles virtue. Anēmeroi (savage, brutal) — a- + hēmeros (tame, cultivated); the savage who has not been civilized.
4: Prodotai (traitors, betrayers) — prodidōmi = to hand over, betray; the one who gives over those who trusted them. Propeteis (rash, reckless) — literally "falling forward," acting impulsively without deliberation. Tetyphōmenoi (puffed up, blinded by smoke) — the perfect passive of typhōō (to fill with smoke/vapor), creating self-inflated blindness; the same verb as 1 Timothy 3:6 and 2 Timothy 6:4. Philēdonoi mallon ē philotheoi (lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God) — the chiastic reversal: philo- compounds define what one loves; the contrast between loving pleasure and loving God is the theological heart of the vice list.
5: Echontes morphōsin eusebeias tēn de dynamin autēs ērnēmenoi (having the form of godliness but denying its power): morphōsis (form, appearance, outward shape) — in Platonic thought the morphē is the form that gives shape; here it is the external religious form without the substance. The vice list of 2 Timothy 3:2-4 is not about openly irreligious people but about those who maintain religious practice (morphōsin eusebeias) while being inwardly corrupt. Apotrepou (turn away from, avoid) — the present imperative of aversion; Timothy is to continuously turn away from such people.
6: Endunontes eis tas oikias kai aichmalōtizontes gynaikaria sesōreumena hamartiais (worming their way into houses and capturing weak women loaded down with sins): endunomai (to creep in, worm in) — the stealthy entry of those who avoid public scrutiny. Gynaikaria — diminutive of gynē (woman); the diminutive may be dismissive (little women) or may indicate socially marginal or young women. Sesōreumena hamartiais (heaped/loaded with sins) — sōreuō = to heap up; the passive image of sins piled onto the person. Agomena epithumiais poikilais (led by various desires) — the varied desires that make them susceptible to manipulation.
7: Pantote manthanonta kai mēdepote eis epignōsin alētheias elthein dynamena (always learning and never able to come to a knowledge of the truth): the irony of perpetual learning without arriving at truth; learning without formation. The characterization applies to those who accumulate teaching from the false teachers without the transformative knowledge that genuine epignōsis alētheias (full knowledge of truth) would produce. Perpetual learning as a substitute for genuine knowledge was a recognized critique in ancient rhetoric — the sophist's audience perpetually sought new teaching rather than living by what they already knew.
8: Hon tropon Iannes kai Iambrēs antestēsan Mōysei (just as Jannes and Jambres opposed Moses): Jannes and Jambres are not named in the OT (Exodus 7:11 refers to "the magicians of Egypt"), but they are named in Jewish tradition: in the Damascus Document (CD 2 Timothy 5:18-19), in Targum Pseudo-Jonathan on Exodus 7:11, and referenced in Origen and other fathers. Their names were widely known in first-century Jewish circles; Paul's reference assumes the audience knows the tradition. The magicians who opposed Moses through counterfeit miracles are the type for those who oppose genuine apostolic teaching through counterfeit wisdom.
9: All' ou prokopsousin epi pleion (but they will not advance further): the irony of prokoptō (advance/progress) used for the false teachers' limited reach; their "progress" is bounded. Hē gar anoia autōn ekdēlos estai pasin (for their folly will be plain to all) — ekdēlos (thoroughly manifest, plain to see) — the prefix ek- intensifies the visibility; what is currently hidden (their true character) will become fully exposed. As with Jannes and Jambres whose folly was exposed when Moses' serpent swallowed theirs, the false teachers' folly has a fixed horizon.
10: Sy de paramēkolouthēkas mou tē didaskalia, tē agōgē, tē prothesei (but you have closely followed/carefully observed my teaching, conduct, purpose): parakoloutheo (to follow alongside, observe closely from proximity) — the intensive prefix para (alongside) + akoloutheo (to follow); the disciple who shadows the teacher, observing not just the words but the entire life. The nine-item list that follows — teaching, conduct, purpose, faith, patience, love, endurance, persecutions, sufferings — is a catalogue of Paul's entire apostolic life as observed by Timothy from close proximity. It is biography as example.
11: Tois diōgmois, tois pathēmasin, hoia moi egeneto en Antiocheia, en Ikoniō, en Lystrois (persecutions, sufferings — what kinds happened to me in Antioch, Iconium, Lystra): the three cities of Paul's first missionary journey (Acts 13-14) where he was stoned, driven out, and left for dead. Timothy was from Lystra (Acts 16:1) — he knew these persecutions not merely by report but by local knowledge and perhaps personal memory. Hoious diōgmous hypēnenka (what persecutions I endured) — hypomenō (to endure under); the Lord's rescue from all (ek pantōn me errysato ho Kyrios) grounds the call to endurance.
12: Pantes de hoi thelontes zēn eusebōs en Christō Iēsou diōchthēsontai (indeed all who want to live godly in Christ Jesus will be persecuted): the universal principle: godly living in Christ produces persecution. Diōchthēsontai (will be persecuted) — future passive; the persecutions are not accidental but the inevitable consequence of the counter-cultural life. This principle contextualizes the specific persecutions Paul listed in 2 Timothy 3:11 as instances of the universal pattern rather than exceptional misfortunes.
13: Ponēroi de anthrōpoi kai goētes prokopsousin epi to cheiron (but evil people and swindlers/impostors will advance to worse): goēs (swindler, impostor, charlatan, juggler) — originally one who uses spells and incantations, then broadly a deceiver. The ironic use of prokoptō (advance/progress) for the errorists' movement from bad to worse: they advance, but in the direction of destruction. Planōntes kai planōmenoi (deceiving and being deceived) — the dual participle: the false teacher who deceives others is simultaneously self-deceived; deception is both inflicted and suffered.
14: Sy de mene en hois emathes kai epistōthēs (but you, remain in what you have learned and have become convinced of): mene (remain, stay, abide) — the aorist imperative of steadfastness; Timothy is to remain fixed while the false teachers advance toward destruction. Epistōthēs (you have become firmly convinced) — the passive of pistoō (to make firm, make reliable); Timothy's conviction has been established by the process of learning from reliable sources. Eidōs para tinōn emathes (knowing from whom you learned) — the source matters; the reliability of the tradition depends on the reliability of the tradents.
15: Kai hoti apo brephous hiera grammata oidas (and that from infancy you have known the holy scriptures): brephos (infant, very young child) — the earliest possible starting point; Timothy's formation in Scripture preceded his own ability to comprehend it (it was passed through his mother and grandmother, 2 Timothy 3:5). Hiera grammata (sacred writings/scriptures) — the Jewish designation for the OT scriptures; this is the only NT use of hiera grammata in distinction from graphē (which appears in 2 Timothy 3:16). The scriptures are described as dynamena se sophisai eis sōtērian (able to make you wise for salvation) — their functional purpose is wisdom-for-salvation rather than abstract knowledge.
17: Hina artios ē ho tou theou anthrōpos, pros pan ergon agathon exērtismenos (so that the person of God may be complete/adequate, fully equipped for every good work): artios (complete, adequate, fitted) — the word for a thing that has all its parts in proper order; the perfect article, the complete vessel. Exērtismenos (fully equipped/furnished) — the perfect passive participle from exartizō, which shares the artios root; the doubling of the artios-root in this verse is deliberate: complete in being and fully equipped in capacity. The purpose of all Scripture's four functions (2 Timothy 3:16) is this single goal: the complete, equipped servant of God.
